data.title: Mark Cuban Says Homes Will Be 'Redesigned Completely' To Accommodate Robots That Could Look 'More Like Ants,' Than Humanoid Robots
data.description:
Mark Cuban isn't buying the hype around humanoid robots. While many companies are racing to build machines that look and move like humans, the billionaire entrepreneur thinks that approach is headed in the wrong direction.

Instead, Cuban says the future will look very different and it could result in homes being built around robots, not the other way around.

Homes Will Be Redesigned For Robots

Speaking on a recent “TBPN” podcast, Cuban pushed back on the idea that robots need to resemble people to be useful. “I think everybody defaults to, well, we live in a human world and humanoids will take the place of humans,” he said. “I think there’s just no chance.”

Don't Miss:

Rather than forcing robots to adapt to human environments, Cuban believes the opposite will happen. “I think houses are going to be redesigned completely so that whatever the optimal robot is that allows it to simplify the house, that’s where houses will go,” he said.

He pointed to warehouses as an example, where companies already use robots designed for specific tasks instead of human-like machines. “They’re not humanoid robots carrying boxes,” Cuban said on “TBPN.” “They’re robots that are designed to fit the environment.” 

In homes, that could mean creating systems where robots operate behind the scenes. Cuban described a setup where appliances like refrigerators and washing machines could be hidden away, with robots handling tasks out of sight. “You could redesign it so all the living space was for people,” he said.

The robots themselves might look nothing like humans. Cuban suggested they could resemble insects instead. “If we had robots that look more like spiders… or more like ants,” he said, describing machines built purely for efficiency and function.

He also pushed back on the long-term viability of humanoid robots altogether. “I think they might have a five-year lifespan and then they’ll fail miserably. Maybe 10,” Cuban told “TPBN,” referring to both the robots and the companies building them.

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

AI Boom Creates Opportunity And Confusion

Cuban’s comments on robotics come as artificial intelligence continues to reshape how businesses operate. He’s seeing a surge of startups building AI agents–software tools that can replace entire job functions like marketing or customer service.

“Agents for everything,” he said on the podcast, describing the current wave. But he added that most of these companies are still early. “They’re just trying to get some traction at all.”

At the same time, AI is already changing how people communicate and work. Cuban told “TPBN” that inboxes are being flooded with AI-generated messages, making it harder to tell who is real. Still, he sees this as a temporary phase as people experiment with new tools.

Despite the noise, he remains optimistic about AI’s long-term impact, especially when it comes to learning.

Why AI Could Be Easier Than The PC Revolution

In a recent post on X, Cuban argued that adapting to AI today is far easier than learning computers during the early PC era.

Back then, access was a major barrier. “A PC cost $4,995, an off brand was $3,995. 5k in 1984 is about $16k today,” he wrote. Learning required manuals or expensive training, which limited who could get ahead.

Today, anyone can open a browser and start learning instantly. “ANYONE can go to their browser, to the AI LLM website of their choice, and type in the words ‘I’m a novice with zero computer background, teach me…'” Cuban wrote.

Cuban also warned that while big companies are likely to cut jobs, “no question about it,” but smaller businesses will need people who understand AI. “Adopt tech quickly, you can outmaneuver big companies,” he said.

As homes and workplaces evolve for robots and AI, investors can tap into the shift through funds tracking automation and tech innovators—giving everyday investors a way to ride the trends shaping tomorrow.

Image: Imagn

data.title: Equinix Has A BBB+ Rating And A 51% Dividend Buffer. The CapEx Cycle Is The Variable Worth Watching.
data.description:
The Stability Case

Equinix sits in a structurally stronger part of the REIT universe. Both S&P and Fitch rate the company BBB+ with a stable outlook, comfortably investment grade and still a few steps removed from the BBB− boundary where funding conditions tend to tighten.

On the numbers, the dividend buffer remains wide. For 2025, Equinix generated AFFO of $38.33 per share. Management has since raised the quarterly dividend to $5.16 per share starting in 2026, or $20.64 on an annualized basis. Using the midpoint of 2026 AFFO guidance — roughly $42.30 per share — the implied buffer sits just above 51%.

In practical terms, AFFO would likely need to decline materially — something in the range of roughly one-third — before the current dividend level fully absorbs cash flow. That is not a thin margin. It is a meaningful cushion.

Leverage is also relatively contained. As of December 31, 2025, net debt to adjusted EBITDA was approximately 3.8x, with a weighted-average debt maturity of roughly 6.5 years. For a global platform operating more than 250 data centers, that combination of moderate leverage and a reasonably long maturity ladder is a structural advantage.

Where Caution Is Warranted

The complication is that Equinix operates one of the more capital-intensive models within the REIT space. Management is guiding to 2026 capex of $3.7–4.2 billion, tied largely to hyperscale demand, AI workloads, and continued xScale expansion.

That level of spending does not really slow down just because financing conditions are less favorable.

AI and cloud demand are doing two things at once. They are supporting bookings, utilization, and forward AFFO growth. At the same time, they are requiring Equinix to keep building — new campuses, incremental capacity, and power infrastructure — which steadily pulls on the balance sheet.

The 51% buffer is real. It just coexists with an investment cycle that is still expanding.

At BBB+ and roughly 3.8x leverage, the company has room to fund that expansion — but not unlimited room. If incremental development is funded more heavily through debt while AFFO per share grows more slowly than capital deployed, the buffer can compress over time, even without any formal change to the dividend itself.

What Would Shift The Assessment

The first variable is leverage trajectory.

Management is currently operating with a target around the high-3x range while funding several billion dollars of annual capex. If that drifts toward 4.5x or higher on a sustained basis, rating-agency language could begin to shift — potentially before any formal rating action occurs.

The second is the relationship between AFFO per share and the development pipeline.

Equinix is guiding to high single-digit AFFO growth in 2026, supported by AI-driven demand and xScale joint ventures. If capital commitments continue to scale faster than per-share cash flow, the buffer likely does not remain static — it compresses gradually.

What I'd Watch

The first is the capex-to-AFFO relationship over the next several reporting periods — specifically, how much incremental AFFO is being reinvested back into growth versus stabilizing leverage.

The second is rating-agency commentary. Agencies rarely move abruptly, but their language tends to adjust before ratings do. Any shift in how leverage tolerance or capital discipline is described would be an early signal that the credit profile is becoming less comfortable.

Equinix's combination of a BBB+ balance sheet, a roughly 51% dividend buffer, and a ~3.8x leverage profile remains a solid starting point for income-credit analysis.

The risk is not a sudden break in dividend safety.
It is a gradual compression of that buffer if capital deployment continues to outpace per-share cash flow growth.

This is not a prediction — it is a structural assessment based on current inputs.

SourceLine: AFFO and dividend figures based on company reporting and management guidance. Credit ratings reflect most recent agency publications. This is not investment advice.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Experts Explain How Retirees Could Benefit From IRA Charitable Changes
data.description:
A bipartisan bill in Congress could give retirees more flexibility in how they donate from their retirement accounts. Tax experts also say timing matters, according to a report by CNBC.

How QCDs Work Today

Under current law, Americans aged 70½ or older can make a qualified charitable distribution, or QCD, a direct transfer from an IRA to an eligible nonprofit. The amount is excluded from taxable income and counts toward required minimum distributions (RMDs), which start at age 73. The 2026 annual limit is $111,000 per individual.

Bill Would Expand Giving Options

The Senate bill, introduced March 3 as a companion to a House measure, would allow QCDs to flow into donor-advised funds (DAFs), which are charitable accounts managed by nonprofits where donors get an up-front tax deduction and can recommend grants over time.

Why QCDs Offer Strong Tax Advantages

Tax attorney Richard Fox said a QCD is “almost always the superior tax move compared to a cash donation.” Because QCDs are excluded from income, they deliver a full 37% marginal tax benefit. Cash donations above $1,000 ($2,000 for joint filers) get no tax benefit for standard deduction filers. QCDs also prevent RMD income from pushing retirees into higher tax brackets, which can raise Medicare premiums.

Concerns Over Delayed Donations

Fox noted that DAFs face no minimum distribution requirement. “The money may stay there for years,” he said. Total DAF assets reached $326.45 billion in 2024, up 27.5% from 2023. Critics worry this could encourage wealth hoarding.

Experts Say Flexibility Could Help

Michael Kenyon, president of the National Association of Charitable Gift Planners, backed the bill, saying it “honors how donors want to give.” The Senate measure is with the Finance Committee; the House bill sits in Ways and Means.

Disclaimer: This content was produced with the help of AI tools and was reviewed and published by Benzinga editors.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: US-Iran War Updates March 25: Iran Seizes Starlink Devices Amid Internet Blackout, Pakistan Reportedly Shares US Demands With Tehran (UPDATED)
data.description:
Here are the latest developments in the U.S.–Israel–Iran war at 3.15 AM ET on Wednesday, as the conflict enters its twenty-sixth day.

Israel and Iran continued to trade airstrikes, while Tehran’s military dismissed President Donald Trump‘s assertion that the United States was engaged in talks to end the war, accusing Washington of essentially “negotiating with itself.”

Iran Rejects Trump’s Claims Amid Reports Of 15-Point Plan

The unified command of the Iranian Armed Forces, which is dominated by the hardline elite Revolutionary Guards (IRGC), rejected Trump’s claims amid reports that the U.S. has sent a 15-point ‌plan for discussion to Tehran through back channels via Pakistan.

“Has the level of your inner struggle reached the stage of you (Trump) negotiating with yourself?” said Ebrahim Zolfaqari, the top spokesperson for Iran’s joint military command, as reported by Reuters.

“As we have always said… no one like us will make a deal with you. Not now. Not ever.”

In a statement to India today, Esmail Baghaei, Iran’s spokesperson for the foreign ministry, said: “We've had a very catastrophic experience, I should say, of the United States diplomacy. We were attacked two times within a span of nine months when we were in the middle of a negotiating process to resolve the nuclear issue. So this was a betrayal of diplomacy.”

Air Attacks Hit Iraq

An Iraqi police source told Al Jazeera that air strikes have targeted Popular Mobilisation Force (PMF) positions in al-Habbaniya in the western Anbar province, but did not specify who carried out the attack.

Hezbollah Intercepts Israeli Fighter Jet In Southern Lebanon

According to Al Jazeera, Lebanese group Hezbollah has said that it intercepted an Israeli jet on Tuesday evening during a potential attack on southern Lebanon, adding that the jet was forced to return after being targeted by surface-to-air missiles.

There has been no comment from Israel yet.

The Shi’ite Muslim group founded by Iran’s Revolutionary Guards in 1982 also said it targeted a military facility in northern Israel on Wednesday.

Pakistan PM Briefs Saudi Crown Prince On Diplomatic Efforts

Pakistan's Prime Minister, Shehbaz Sharif, stated that he has been in contact with Mohammed bin Salman, reiterating Islamabad's "strong condemnation" of the recent attacks on Saudi Arabia.

In a post on X, Sharif commended Saudi Arabia for its "remarkable restraint" during the crisis and called for an "immediate cessation of hostilities," a return to normal conditions, and enhanced unity among Muslim nations.

He added, "I also briefed His Royal Highness on Pakistan's diplomatic initiatives aimed at promoting regional peace and stability."

This exchange comes as Pakistan seeks to position itself as a potential venue for US-Iran talks amid the ongoing conflict. Sharif emphasized on Tuesday that Pakistan "stands ready and honored to host meaningful and conclusive discussions to achieve a comprehensive resolution of the ongoing conflict."

Fire Erupts As Drone Attack Hits Fuel Tank At Kuwait International Airport

Drones targeted a ​fuel tank ‌at Kuwait International Airport, ​causing a ​fire but no ⁠casualties, ​Kuwait’s Civil Aviation ​Authority said. Emergency procedures ​were ​activated immediately, with firefighting ‌teams ⁠responding to the blaze, adding that it caused only “limited” damage and no casualties.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: The Tesla Brand Has Hit A Bottom — And It Has An Elon Musk Problem
data.description:
The contrast is stark — and increasingly hard to ignore.

• What’s next for TSLA stock?

Tesla: Category Leader To Perception Laggard

The report doesn't mince words: "Tesla remains at the bottom of the pack," while "Toyota and Honda continue to lead on brand positivity and trust."

Despite near-universal recognition, Tesla posts a brand positively score of -4 (versus +20 and +16, respectively) and a brand trust score of -8 (versus +26 and +20, respectively), placing it behind both legacy automakers and newer EV players.

In other words, people know Tesla — but they don't necessarily trust it.

The Problem Isn't Just The Product

Here's where it gets more nuanced — and more uncomfortable.

The report highlights that when consumers think about Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) technology, they aren't just thinking about autonomy.

They're thinking about Elon Musk.

"Elon Musk ranks among the top associations, and negative Musk sentiment accounts for 14% of responses — more than double the share citing unfamiliarity with the Tesla technology."

That's a critical distinction.

This isn't broad brand sentiment. It's specifically tied to FSD, one of Tesla's most important long-term bets. And the implication is clear: concerns around the technology are being shaped not just by performance or safety, but by who's leading it.

When Elon Musk Becomes A Variable

Digging deeper into the FSD data, "Negative Elon Musk Association" emerges as a distinct response category at 14% —alongside “Safety Concerns & Danger” at 33% and “General Dislike & Opposition” at 17%.

That puts Musk directly into the perception equation. Not as background noise — but as a measurable factor.

The Bigger Picture

Tesla still dominates the EV conversation. But the data suggests the narrative may be shifting — from product leadership to perception risk.

And if brand trust continues to lag while visibility stays high, that gap doesn't just linger. It compounds.
